claims.claim_text: Google's incident management approach is based on the Incident Command System, which originated in emergency-response/firefighting practice.
claims.rationale: The claim appears to be real and specifically documented in Google's SRE workbook, but the primary source is off the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Most incidents are SEV-3 or SEV-4.
claims.anchor_text: Most incidents are SEV-3 or SEV-4.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Many incidents turn out to be lower-severity issues rather than true SEV-1 emergencies.
claims.rationale: This is a frequency claim with no cited dataset and should be rewritten to remove the unsupported quantifier.

claims.claim_text: Severity should be classified correctly within 5 minutes.
claims.anchor_text: **Goal**: Correct severity classification within 5 minutes.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Goal**: Classify severity quickly enough to involve the right responders and escalation path.
claims.rationale: The exact five-minute target is an uncited operational benchmark and should be generalized unless backed by a named standard.

claims.claim_text: On-call stretches should be capped at one week.
claims.anchor_text: - Maximum 1 week on-call stretches
claims.suggested_rewrite: - Keep on-call stretches short enough to avoid chronic fatigue
claims.rationale: The one-week cap is a specific policy recommendation presented without a cited source and is organization-dependent.

claims.claim_text: An on-call rotation needs at least two people.
claims.anchor_text: - Minimum 2 people in rotation
claims.suggested_rewrite: - Have enough people in the rotation that one person is not carrying on-call alone
claims.rationale: The minimum headcount is an uncited staffing threshold and should be reframed as a general resilience principle.

claims.claim_text: A good target is fewer than five pages per on-call week.
claims.anchor_text: | Pages per on-call week | < 5 | More = burnout |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Pages per on-call week | Track the trend over time | Sustained page volume drives fatigue and burnout |
claims.rationale: The numeric threshold is an uncited benchmark that varies by team and service design.

claims.claim_text: A good false-positive target is under 20%.
claims.anchor_text: | False positive rate | < 20% | Higher = fatigue |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | False positive rate | Keep it low enough that responders trust alerts | Higher rates train people to ignore pages |
claims.rationale: The specific percentage is presented as a benchmark without a cited source.

claims.claim_text: A good time-to-acknowledge target is under five minutes.
claims.anchor_text: | Time to acknowledge | < 5 min | Faster = faster response |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Time to acknowledge | Minutes, not tens of minutes | Faster acknowledgment usually shortens response |
claims.rationale: The exact five-minute threshold is an uncited SLA-style benchmark rather than a universally established fact.

claims.claim_text: A good target is fewer than 20% of incidents requiring escalation.
claims.anchor_text: | Incidents requiring escalation | < 20% | Higher = skill gaps |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Incidents requiring escalation | Track and review by team maturity | Frequent escalation can reveal coverage or skill gaps |
claims.rationale: The percentage is an uncited benchmark and is too precise to leave as a fact.

claims.claim_text: A good on-call satisfaction target is above 3/5.
claims.anchor_text: | On-call satisfaction | > 3/5 | Lower = retention risk |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | On-call satisfaction | Track regularly with your team | Sustained dissatisfaction is a retention risk |
claims.rationale: The score threshold is an uncited people-metric benchmark and should be generalized.

claims.claim_text: All-hands incidents often have worse outcomes than properly staffed responses.
claims.anchor_text: 3. **"All hands" incidents often have worse outcomes** than properly staffed responses. Too many people creates confusion and duplicated effort.
claims.suggested_rewrite: 3. Large, unstructured response groups can create confusion and duplicated effort, so major incidents still need clear role assignment.
claims.rationale: The comparative outcome claim is uncited and overly broad, but the operational lesson can be preserved without asserting measured outcomes.

claims.claim_text: PagerDuty's annual State of On-Call report consistently shows that high-performing organizations page engineers less often but with more complex issues.
claims.anchor_text: 4. **PagerDuty's annual "State of On-Call" report** consistently shows that engineers at high-performing organizations get paged less frequently but handle more complex issues—because they've automated away the simple stuff and have better tooling for the hard stuff.
claims.suggested_rewrite: 4. High-performing organizations often reduce low-value pages by automating routine issues and improving their tooling, leaving responders to focus on the harder problems.
claims.rationale: The module attributes a specific recurring finding to a named vendor report without a directly matching citable source.

claims.claim_text: The section is framed as a real war story rather than an illustrative example.
claims.anchor_text: ## War Story: The Incident That Went Right
claims.suggested_rewrite: ## Example: A Well-Run Incident Response
claims.rationale: The heading presents an uncited anecdote as a real incident and should be reframed as an example.

claims.claim_text: The author personally worked with a company that experienced the described outage.
claims.anchor_text: A company I worked with had a major outage:
claims.suggested_rewrite: Consider a representative outage pattern:
claims.rationale: This personal-attribution setup turns the following uncited scenario into a factual anecdote.

claims.claim_text: The outage started at 2:30 AM and took the whole site down.
claims.anchor_text: - 2:30 AM: Total site down
claims.suggested_rewrite: - Early morning: the site goes fully down
claims.rationale: The exact timestamp is false precision in an unsourced anecdote.

claims.claim_text: The outage was caused by database corruption from a failed migration.
claims.anchor_text: - Cause: Database corruption from failed migration
claims.suggested_rewrite: - Cause: a failed migration corrupts critical database state
claims.rationale: The section needs the operational lesson, not an uncited claim that this exact cause occurred in a real incident.

claims.claim_text: At 2:35 AM the incident commander opened an incident channel.
claims.anchor_text: 2:35 AM: IC opens incident channel
claims.suggested_rewrite: Shortly after detection: IC opens incident channel
claims.rationale: The exact minute-by-minute timeline is uncited false precision in a purported real incident.

claims.claim_text: At 2:40 AM Comms posted an investigating update to the status page.
claims.anchor_text: 2:40 AM: Comms posts to status page: "Investigating issues"
claims.suggested_rewrite: Soon after: Comms posts an initial status-page update
claims.rationale: This is another uncited exact timeline detail that should be generalized.

claims.claim_text: At 2:50 AM the Tech Lead identified database corruption.
claims.anchor_text: 2:50 AM: Tech Lead: "Identified - database corruption"
claims.suggested_rewrite: After initial investigation: Tech Lead identifies database corruption
claims.rationale: The exact time and quote-like phrasing make this an unsupported anecdotal claim.

claims.claim_text: At 3:00 AM Comms updated the status page with an ETA.
claims.anchor_text: 3:00 AM: Comms updates status page with ETA
claims.suggested_rewrite: Once the team has a plan: Comms updates the status page with an ETA
claims.rationale: The specific timestamp is not supportable as written and should be generalized.

claims.claim_text: At 3:30 AM the database was restored from backup.
claims.anchor_text: 3:30 AM: Database restored from backup
claims.suggested_rewrite: After the restore decision: the database is recovered from backup
claims.rationale: The exact recovery time is uncited and unnecessary to preserve the lesson.

claims.claim_text: At 3:45 AM the site came back online.
claims.anchor_text: 3:45 AM: Site back online
claims.suggested_rewrite: Recovery follows soon after: the site comes back online
claims.rationale: The minute-level restoration claim is unsourced anecdotal precision.

claims.claim_text: At 4:00 AM the incident commander declared the incident resolved.
claims.anchor_text: 4:00 AM: IC declares incident resolved
claims.suggested_rewrite: After stability checks: the IC declares the incident resolved
claims.rationale: The exact resolution timestamp is part of the same unsupported anecdotal timeline.

claims.claim_text: The incident caused 75 minutes of total downtime.
claims.anchor_text: - 75 minutes total downtime
claims.suggested_rewrite: - Downtime lasts a little over an hour
claims.rationale: The precise duration is an uncited quantitative claim in an unattributed war story.

claims.claim_text: A similar issue six months earlier caused four hours of chaotic response.
claims.anchor_text: - Similar issue 6 months earlier: 4 hours of chaos
claims.suggested_rewrite: - A comparable earlier incident had taken much longer and felt far less coordinated
claims.rationale: This compares two uncited incidents using exact durations and dates that cannot be verified from the module.

further_reading.title: NIST SP 800-61 Rev. 3
further_reading.why_relevant: Authoritative guidance on incident-response preparation, coordination, response, and post-incident improvement.

further_reading.title: Prometheus Alerting Rules
further_reading.why_relevant: Useful for designing actionable alerts and understanding pending versus firing behavior in real incident-response systems.

further_reading.title: Visual Guide to Incident Metrics
further_reading.why_relevant: Practical reference for incident timelines and core metrics such as detection, response, mitigation, and recovery.

notes: The shared section pool is mostly Prometheus/Kubernetes capacity material and did not directly cover the module's historical incident-management claims, quantitative on-call targets, or the uncited war-story section.
